title: Dyeing with hawthorn berries
subtitle: A pale pink to soft rose on alum-mordanted wool from autumn haws
excerpt: Hawthorn berries (haws) yield a soft pink to rose on alum-mordanted wool when used as a cold-extraction or low-heat dye bath. The colour is gentle and not highly lightfast, so it is best suited to indoor decorative items rather than hardwearing textiles.
sourceNotes: Hawthorn berry dyeing synthesised from general British foraging and natural dye practice. Haws contain anthocyanins that produce pinks and purples; these are less lightfast than flavonoid yellows and tannin browns. Low-heat extraction preserves more of the anthocyanin than simmering.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt used to fix dye to the fibre. Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the standard mordant for berry dyes and helps fix the pink colour more firmly than unmordanted fibre.
A group of red and purple plant pigments found in berries, red cabbage, and other plant sources. Anthocyanins shift colour with pH: acidic conditions give pink, alkaline conditions give purple-grey.
The resistance of a colour to fading under light. Anthocyanin-based colours have low to moderate lightfastness; store or display finished pieces away from direct sunlight.

content.text: Hawthorn berries give a soft, fugitive pink on alum-mordanted wool. The
pigments are pH-sensitive: an acidic dye bath (add a splash of white vinegar) gives a clearer pink, while a neutral or slightly alkaline bath gives grey-rose.
is low to moderate. Collect the berries in October when they are fully red and firm.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Use dedicated dye-pots. Wear gloves and apron. Alum is low-hazard; keep away from food.

content.text: Mordant with 15 g alum as described in the standard alum pre-mordant method: dissolve in hot water, add to the dye-pot with water, add pre-wetted wool, raise to 85°C, hold 45 minutes. Allow to cool and remove the wool.

Crush the haws lightly and place in a clean dye-pot with cold water. Add the white vinegar to acidify the bath. Heat slowly to 60°C only; do not simmer. Simmering anthocyanin dyes shifts them toward brown-grey and reduces the pink. Hold at 55 to 60°C for 45 minutes, then strain out the berries.

-treated, damp wool to the strained, acidified dye bath. Keep the temperature at 55 to 60°C for 45 minutes. Rinse in water of the same temperature, then progressively cooler water. Dry in shade and store away from direct light.
The alum mordant bath and berry dye bath can be composted or disposed of via the foul drain. Neither presents significant hazard at home quantities.

items.problem: The result is grey-brown rather than pink
items.cause: The dye bath was too hot, or no acid was added to the bath
items.fix: Keep the bath below 65°C and add white vinegar to maintain an acid pH. Check with pH strips: a reading of 4 to 5 gives the best pink result.
